name: Psychology Foundations
description: A foundational survey of core psychological concepts spanning brain science, cognition, development, personality, and mental health for beginning students.

modules.title: Introduction to Psychology
modules.objectives: Define psychology as a science, trace its historical roots, identify major theoretical perspectives, and describe career paths in the field.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — History of Psychology Practice Quiz
modules.assignment: Write a 300-word reflection identifying which psychological perspective (biological, psychodynamic, behavioral, cognitive, or humanistic) best explains a behavior you have observed in daily life. Justify your choice with at least two specific reasons.

modules.title: Brain & Nervous System
modules.objectives: Describe the structure and function of neurons, explain synaptic transmission, and identify the roles of major brain regions in behavior and mental processes.
modules.assignment: Create a labeled diagram of the human brain identifying the four cortical lobes, the limbic system structures, and the brainstem. Write one sentence describing the primary function of each labeled structure.

modules.title: Sensation & Perception
modules.objectives: Distinguish sensation from perception, apply Weber's Law and signal detection theory, and explain key Gestalt principles and top-down vs. bottom-up processing.
modules.assignment: Analyze three optical illusions and explain each using a specific principle of perceptual psychology such as figure-ground, closure, or size constancy. Include a sketch or printed image of each illusion.

modules.title: States of Consciousness
modules.objectives: Define consciousness, describe the stages of sleep and their functions, and explain the effects of psychoactive drugs on brain and behavior.
modules.assignment: Keep a sleep journal for five days recording your estimated sleep hours, sleep quality, and any dreams recalled. Analyze your data and connect it to at least two concepts from the module such as REM rebound or sleep debt.

modules.title: Learning Theories
modules.objectives: Explain classical and operant conditioning with real-life examples, compare reinforcement schedules and their effects, and describe observational learning.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Classical & Operant Conditioning Practice
modules.assignment: Design a behavior-modification plan using operant conditioning principles to increase a positive behavior (such as studying or exercise). Specify the reinforcement type, schedule, and a plan for fading reinforcement over time.

modules.title: Memory Systems
modules.objectives: Describe the three-stage model of memory, explain encoding, storage, and retrieval processes, and identify common reasons for forgetting.
modules.assignment: Test yourself on a list of 20 random words immediately and again after 24 hours. Record your scores and write a 250-word analysis connecting your results to the serial position effect, forgetting curve, and one strategy for improving memory retention.

modules.title: Thinking & Language
modules.objectives: Describe cognitive processes involved in problem-solving and decision-making, explain common cognitive biases and heuristics, and describe the relationship between language and thought.
modules.assignment: Identify three cognitive biases you have personally experienced (such as confirmation bias, availability heuristic, or anchoring). Describe each situation and explain how the bias influenced your decision or judgment.

modules.title: Intelligence
modules.objectives: Define intelligence, compare major theories including Spearman's g, Gardner's multiple intelligences, and Sternberg's triarchic theory, and evaluate the role of heredity and environment in intelligence.
modules.assignment: Compare Gardner's multiple intelligences theory and Spearman's g factor. In a 300-word essay, argue which theory better accounts for academic and real-world success, providing at least two pieces of supporting evidence for your position.

modules.title: Motivation & Emotion
modules.objectives: Compare theories of motivation including Maslow's hierarchy, drive-reduction, and self-determination theory, and explain major theories of emotion.
modules.assignment: Apply Maslow's hierarchy of needs to a real or fictional character (from a book, film, or current events). Identify which level of the hierarchy is currently motivating the character's behavior and explain your reasoning in 250 words.

modules.title: Child Development
modules.objectives: Describe physical, cognitive, and socioemotional milestones in infancy and childhood, apply Piaget's stages, and explain attachment theory.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Piaget's Stages of Cognitive Development
modules.assignment: Observe or recall interactions with a child under the age of 10. Using Piaget's theory, identify which cognitive stage the child appears to be in and provide three specific behavioral examples that support your conclusion.

modules.title: Adolescent Development
modules.objectives: Describe the physical, cognitive, and psychosocial changes of adolescence, apply Erikson's identity vs. role confusion stage, and explain factors that influence peer relationships.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Erikson's Stages of Psychosocial Development
modules.assignment: Write a 300-word personal essay reflecting on your own experience with Erikson's identity vs. role confusion stage. Identify two influences that have shaped your sense of identity and connect them to specific concepts from the module.

modules.title: Personality Overview
modules.objectives: Compare the psychodynamic, humanistic, trait, and social-cognitive theories of personality, and critically evaluate personality assessment methods.
modules.assignment: Rate yourself on each of the Big Five personality dimensions (Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Neuroticism) on a 1–10 scale. Write a 300-word reflection discussing how two of these traits have influenced your academic or social life.

modules.title: Social Behavior
modules.objectives: Explain key social psychology concepts including conformity, obedience, attitudes, attribution, and prosocial and antisocial behavior.
modules.assignment: Describe a real-life situation in which you experienced or observed conformity or obedience. Apply either Asch's conformity research or Milgram's obedience study to analyze the situation, and propose what factors might have led to a different outcome.

modules.title: Psychological Health
modules.objectives: Define psychological disorders using the DSM criteria, describe major categories of disorders, and explain evidence-based treatment approaches including psychotherapy and medication.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Overview of Mental Health Treatment
modules.assignment: Research one psychological disorder from the DSM-5 (such as major depressive disorder, generalized anxiety disorder, or PTSD). Write a 350-word summary covering its diagnostic criteria, prevalence, biological and psychological causes, and the most effective evidence-based treatments.

quizQuestions.question: Which perspective focuses on unconscious drives and early childhood experiences as determinants of behavior?
quizQuestions.explanation: The psychodynamic perspective, rooted in Freud's work, emphasizes unconscious processes and early life experiences.

quizQuestions.question: Wilhelm Wundt is best known for establishing the first scientific:
quizQuestions.explanation: Wundt opened the first experimental psychology laboratory in Leipzig, Germany in 1879.

quizQuestions.question: Which psychological perspective emphasizes free will and human potential for growth?
quizQuestions.explanation: Humanistic psychology, associated with Maslow and Rogers, focuses on personal growth and self-actualization.

quizQuestions.question: Psychology is formally defined as the scientific study of:
quizQuestions.explanation: Modern psychology is the scientific study of both observable behavior and internal mental processes.

quizQuestions.question: Structuralism, the first formal school of psychology, attempted to analyze:
quizQuestions.explanation: Structuralists used introspection to break down conscious experience into its basic components.

quizQuestions.question: Which part of the neuron receives incoming signals from other neurons?
quizQuestions.explanation: Dendrites are branch-like structures that receive incoming signals from other neurons.

quizQuestions.question: The process by which a neuron fires an electrical signal is called:
quizQuestions.explanation: An action potential is the electrical impulse that travels down the axon when a neuron fires.

quizQuestions.question: Which brain structure is primarily responsible for processing fear and emotional memories?
quizQuestions.explanation: The amygdala plays a central role in processing fear, threat detection, and emotional memories.

quizQuestions.question: Dopamine and serotonin are examples of:
quizQuestions.explanation: Neurotransmitters are chemical messengers that transmit signals across the synapse between neurons.

quizQuestions.question: The cerebral cortex's frontal lobe is most associated with:
quizQuestions.explanation: The prefrontal cortex within the frontal lobe governs planning, decision-making, and impulse control.

quizQuestions.question: Weber's Law states that the just noticeable difference between two stimuli is:
options: A constant proportion of the original stimulus
quizQuestions.answer: A constant proportion of the original stimulus
quizQuestions.explanation: Weber's Law holds that the JND is a constant fraction of the stimulus intensity.

quizQuestions.question: The process by which sensory receptors convert physical energy into neural signals is called:
quizQuestions.explanation: Transduction is the conversion of physical stimuli (light, sound) into electrochemical neural signals.

quizQuestions.question: Which Gestalt principle explains why we perceive a row of dots as a line?
quizQuestions.explanation: The Gestalt principle of proximity states that objects close together are perceived as a group or line.

quizQuestions.question: Top-down processing involves:
options: Building perception from raw sensory data
Using prior knowledge and expectations to interpret stimuli
Processing only the loudest auditory signals
quizQuestions.answer: Using prior knowledge and expectations to interpret stimuli
quizQuestions.explanation: Top-down processing is driven by cognition — our prior knowledge shapes how we interpret incoming sensory information.

quizQuestions.question: Sensory adaptation refers to the tendency of sensory receptors to:
options: Decrease responding to an unchanging stimulus
quizQuestions.answer: Decrease responding to an unchanging stimulus
quizQuestions.explanation: Sensory adaptation is the diminished sensitivity to a constant stimulus, allowing us to focus on changes.

quizQuestions.question: REM sleep is primarily associated with:
options: Slow delta waves and deep rest
quizQuestions.explanation: REM (rapid eye movement) sleep is when vivid dreaming occurs and is linked to memory consolidation.

quizQuestions.question: Which theory proposes that we sleep to consolidate memories and restore neural connections?
quizQuestions.explanation: The information-processing theory of sleep suggests sleep helps consolidate and organize daily experiences into memory.

quizQuestions.question: A drug that mimics or increases the effect of a neurotransmitter is called an:
quizQuestions.explanation: Agonists bind to receptor sites and mimic or enhance the action of a neurotransmitter.

quizQuestions.question: Which stage of NREM sleep is characterized by slow delta waves and is hardest to wake from?
quizQuestions.explanation: Stage 3 NREM (slow-wave sleep) features delta waves and is the deepest, most restorative sleep stage.

quizQuestions.question: Alcohol is classified as which type of psychoactive drug?
quizQuestions.explanation: Alcohol is a CNS depressant that slows neural activity and reduces inhibition.

quizQuestions.question: In classical conditioning, the neutral stimulus that comes to elicit a response after pairing is called the:
quizQuestions.explanation: After repeated pairings with the UCS, the neutral stimulus becomes the conditioned stimulus (CS).

quizQuestions.question: Thorndike's Law of Effect states that behaviors followed by satisfying consequences are:
quizQuestions.explanation: The Law of Effect is the foundation of operant conditioning: rewarded behaviors increase in frequency.

quizQuestions.question: A variable-ratio reinforcement schedule produces:
options: High rates of responding resistant to extinction
Responding only at fixed time intervals
quizQuestions.answer: High rates of responding resistant to extinction
quizQuestions.explanation: Variable-ratio schedules (like slot machines) produce the highest response rates and strongest resistance to extinction.

quizQuestions.question: Bandura's Bobo doll experiments demonstrated:
quizQuestions.explanation: Bandura showed that children imitate aggressive behavior they observe in models, demonstrating observational (social) learning.

quizQuestions.question: Negative reinforcement involves:
options: Adding an unpleasant stimulus to decrease behavior
Removing an aversive stimulus to increase behavior
quizQuestions.answer: Removing an aversive stimulus to increase behavior
quizQuestions.explanation: Negative reinforcement strengthens a behavior by removing an unpleasant stimulus when the behavior occurs.

quizQuestions.question: In Atkinson-Shiffrin's model, information that is not attended to is lost from:
quizQuestions.explanation: Sensory memory holds information briefly; without attention it decays and is never transferred to short-term memory.

quizQuestions.question: Miller's Law states that short-term memory can hold approximately how many chunks of information?
quizQuestions.explanation: George Miller's famous paper proposed that STM capacity is 7 ± 2 chunks of information.

quizQuestions.question: The phenomenon where items at the beginning of a list are best recalled is called the:
quizQuestions.explanation: The primacy effect occurs because early list items have had more time for rehearsal and transfer to LTM.

quizQuestions.question: Procedural memory (knowing how to ride a bike) is a type of:
quizQuestions.explanation: Procedural memory is implicit — it operates without conscious awareness and underlies skills and habits.

quizQuestions.question: Ebbinghaus's forgetting curve shows that memory loss is greatest:
quizQuestions.explanation: The forgetting curve shows rapid initial forgetting that slows over time — most is lost within the first hour.

quizQuestions.question: The tendency to search for information that confirms our existing beliefs is called:
quizQuestions.explanation: Confirmation bias leads people to favor information that supports their existing views and ignore contradictory evidence.

quizQuestions.question: The availability heuristic leads people to judge likelihood based on:
options: How easily an example comes to mind
quizQuestions.answer: How easily an example comes to mind
quizQuestions.explanation: The availability heuristic is a mental shortcut where we assess probability by how quickly examples are recalled.

quizQuestions.question: Chomsky's concept of 'language acquisition device' suggests that language ability is:
options: Innately built into the human brain
quizQuestions.answer: Innately built into the human brain
quizQuestions.explanation: Chomsky proposed that humans have an innate biological mechanism (LAD) that facilitates language acquisition.

quizQuestions.question: The Whorfian (linguistic relativity) hypothesis suggests that:
options: Language influences how we perceive and think about the world
quizQuestions.answer: Language influences how we perceive and think about the world
quizQuestions.explanation: Linguistic relativity proposes that the language we speak shapes our perception and categorization of experience.

quizQuestions.question: Mental set refers to the tendency to:
options: Use the first solution that comes to mind
Apply a previously successful problem-solving strategy even when inappropriate
quizQuestions.answer: Apply a previously successful problem-solving strategy even when inappropriate
quizQuestions.explanation: Mental set is a cognitive rigidity in which people persist with strategies that worked before, even when they are ineffective.

quizQuestions.question: Spearman's 'g' factor refers to:
quizQuestions.explanation: Spearman's g is a general intelligence factor he believed underlies performance on all cognitive tasks.

quizQuestions.question: Gardner's theory of multiple intelligences includes which of the following types?
quizQuestions.explanation: Gardner proposed eight intelligences including linguistic, logical-mathematical, spatial, musical, and bodily-kinesthetic among others.

quizQuestions.question: The Flynn Effect refers to:
options: Rising average IQ scores across generations
quizQuestions.answer: Rising average IQ scores across generations
quizQuestions.explanation: The Flynn Effect is the documented rise in average IQ scores observed across many nations over the 20th century.

quizQuestions.question: Fluid intelligence refers to the ability to:
options: Apply previously learned knowledge and skills
quizQuestions.explanation: Fluid intelligence is the capacity to think logically and solve new problems independent of acquired knowledge.

quizQuestions.question: An IQ score of 100 represents:
options: The average score for a given age group
quizQuestions.answer: The average score for a given age group
quizQuestions.explanation: IQ tests are normed so that 100 represents the average performance for a given age group.

quizQuestions.question: According to Maslow's hierarchy, which need must generally be met before esteem needs?
quizQuestions.explanation: In Maslow's hierarchy, belonging/love (social) needs must be met before esteem needs can be pursued.

quizQuestions.question: The James-Lange theory of emotion proposes that emotions result from:
options: The thalamus triggering both body and experience simultaneously
quizQuestions.explanation: James-Lange theory: we feel afraid because we notice we are trembling; the bodily response precedes the emotion.

quizQuestions.question: Intrinsic motivation refers to engaging in an activity:
options: To earn a reward or avoid punishment
Because it is inherently interesting or enjoyable
quizQuestions.answer: Because it is inherently interesting or enjoyable
quizQuestions.explanation: Intrinsic motivation comes from internal interest and enjoyment rather than external rewards.

quizQuestions.question: The Cannon-Bard theory states that emotion and physiological arousal occur:
quizQuestions.explanation: Cannon-Bard proposed that emotion-triggering stimuli simultaneously produce both the subjective emotion and physiological arousal.

quizQuestions.question: Self-Determination Theory identifies autonomy, competence, and relatedness as:
options: Basic psychological needs for intrinsic motivation
quizQuestions.answer: Basic psychological needs for intrinsic motivation
quizQuestions.explanation: Deci & Ryan's SDT argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are universal psychological needs supporting intrinsic motivation.

quizQuestions.question: According to Piaget, object permanence develops during the:
quizQuestions.explanation: Object permanence — knowing objects exist when out of sight — emerges around 8 months during the sensorimotor stage.

quizQuestions.question: Harlow's monkey studies demonstrated the importance of:
options: Food as the primary attachment motivator
quizQuestions.explanation: Harlow's infant monkeys preferred the cloth 'mother' for comfort over the wire 'mother' with food, showing contact comfort drives attachment.

quizQuestions.question: The ability to understand that quantity remains constant despite changes in appearance is called:
quizQuestions.explanation: Conservation, which develops in the concrete operational stage, is the understanding that amount doesn't change when shape changes.

quizQuestions.question: Which type of attachment is characterized by distress when the caregiver leaves and anger upon return?
quizQuestions.explanation: Ambivalent attachment features intense separation distress and conflicted behavior (anger and seeking comfort) upon reunion.

quizQuestions.question: Vygotsky's zone of proximal development refers to:
options: The gap between what a child can do alone vs. with guidance
The biological timetable for cognitive development
The age range for formal operations
The difference between IQ and academic achievement
quizQuestions.answer: The gap between what a child can do alone vs. with guidance
quizQuestions.explanation: The ZPD is the range of tasks a child can accomplish with skilled guidance but not yet independently.

quizQuestions.question: Erikson's psychosocial stage for adolescence is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Erikson's fifth stage occurs during adolescence, where the central challenge is forming a coherent personal identity.

quizQuestions.question: The imaginary audience phenomenon, common in adolescence, refers to:
options: Teens imagining they are being constantly watched and judged by others
Peer pressure to conform to group norms
Believing one's experiences are unique and special
quizQuestions.answer: Teens imagining they are being constantly watched and judged by others
quizQuestions.explanation: Elkind described the imaginary audience as adolescent egocentrism — the belief that others are preoccupied with one's behavior.

quizQuestions.question: Which brain region is last to fully mature, contributing to adolescent risk-taking?
quizQuestions.explanation: The prefrontal cortex, responsible for impulse control and planning, is not fully myelinated until the mid-20s.

quizQuestions.question: Marcia's identity statuses include all of the following EXCEPT:
quizQuestions.explanation: Marcia identified four statuses: achievement, foreclosure, moratorium, and diffusion. Identity regression is not one of them.

quizQuestions.question: The secular trend in puberty refers to:
options: Later onset of puberty over time
Earlier onset of puberty across generations
Puberty occurring at the same age globally
The social influences on puberty timing
quizQuestions.answer: Earlier onset of puberty across generations
quizQuestions.explanation: The secular trend documents that puberty has been occurring at progressively younger ages over the past century, likely due to nutrition.

quizQuestions.question: The Big Five personality model includes all EXCEPT:
quizQuestions.explanation: The Big Five are Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, and Neuroticism. Psychoticism is from Eysenck's model.

quizQuestions.question: Freud's concept of the ego operates according to the:
quizQuestions.explanation: The ego mediates between the id's pleasure principle and external reality, operating on the reality principle.

quizQuestions.question: Maslow and Rogers are associated with which personality perspective?
quizQuestions.explanation: Both Maslow and Rogers are central figures in humanistic psychology, emphasizing growth and self-actualization.

quizQuestions.question: Bandura's concept of self-efficacy refers to:
options: Belief in one's ability to succeed at a specific task
quizQuestions.answer: Belief in one's ability to succeed at a specific task
quizQuestions.explanation: Self-efficacy is the belief in one's own capacity to execute behaviors needed to achieve specific goals.

quizQuestions.question: The Rorschach inkblot test is an example of a:
quizQuestions.explanation: Projective tests like the Rorschach present ambiguous stimuli and ask subjects to project meaning, revealing unconscious content.

quizQuestions.question: Asch's conformity experiments demonstrated that people often conform due to:
quizQuestions.explanation: Asch's subjects conformed to obviously wrong answers to avoid standing out — driven by normative (social approval) pressure.

quizQuestions.question: The fundamental attribution error refers to the tendency to:
options: Overestimate situational factors in explaining others' behavior
Overestimate dispositional factors in explaining others' behavior
quizQuestions.answer: Overestimate dispositional factors in explaining others' behavior
quizQuestions.explanation: FAE is the bias to explain others' actions by their character/traits while underestimating situational influences.

quizQuestions.question: Milgram's obedience experiments showed that ordinary people would administer apparent electric shocks primarily because:
options: They did not understand the situation
quizQuestions.explanation: Milgram's research demonstrated that perceived authority from a legitimate source (experimenter) caused high obedience.

quizQuestions.question: The bystander effect predicts that the probability of helping in an emergency:
options: Increases as more bystanders are present
Decreases as more bystanders are present
Is unaffected by the number of bystanders
quizQuestions.answer: Decreases as more bystanders are present
quizQuestions.explanation: The bystander effect (Latané & Darley) shows diffusion of responsibility reduces helping as group size increases.

quizQuestions.question: Cognitive dissonance occurs when:
options: Two people disagree about a belief
A person holds two conflicting cognitions simultaneously
quizQuestions.answer: A person holds two conflicting cognitions simultaneously
quizQuestions.explanation: Cognitive dissonance (Festinger) is the discomfort from holding contradictory beliefs, motivating attitude or behavior change.

quizQuestions.question: The DSM-5 is used to:
quizQuestions.explanation: The DSM-5 (Diagnostic and Statistical Manual) provides criteria for classifying and diagnosing mental disorders.

quizQuestions.question: Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy (CBT) targets:
quizQuestions.explanation: CBT is a structured, evidence-based therapy that challenges and changes unhelpful thought patterns and behaviors.

quizQuestions.question: Major depressive disorder is characterized by:
options: Alternating episodes of mania and depression
Persistent depressed mood and loss of interest for at least two weeks
Sudden intense fear without a known cause
Preoccupation with illness and bodily symptoms
quizQuestions.answer: Persistent depressed mood and loss of interest for at least two weeks
quizQuestions.explanation: MDD requires depressed mood or anhedonia most of the day, nearly every day, for a minimum of two weeks.

quizQuestions.question: Which neurotransmitter is most associated with mood regulation and is targeted by SSRIs?
quizQuestions.explanation: SSRIs (selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors) increase serotonin availability, the neurotransmitter most linked to mood disorders.

quizQuestions.question: Schizophrenia's positive symptoms include:
quizQuestions.explanation: Positive symptoms of schizophrenia are excesses added to normal behavior, including hallucinations, delusions, and disorganized speech.

questions.question: Which early school of psychology used introspection to study the basic elements of consciousness?
questions.explanation: Wundt and Titchener's structuralism used introspection to analyze the components of conscious experience.

questions.question: Which neurotransmitter is most associated with the reward pathway and motivation?
questions.explanation: Dopamine is central to the brain's reward system, regulating motivation, pleasure, and reinforcement learning.

questions.question: The absolute threshold is defined as the minimum stimulus intensity detected:
questions.explanation: The absolute threshold is the stimulus level detected 50% of the time under ideal conditions.

questions.question: Sleep deprivation is most likely to impair which type of memory?
questions.explanation: Sleep, especially REM and slow-wave sleep, is critical for consolidating declarative (explicit) memories.

questions.question: Extinction in classical conditioning occurs when:
options: The CS is repeatedly presented without the UCS
The UCS is presented without the CS
Reinforcement is removed from operant behavior
The organism generalizes the CS to other stimuli
questions.answer: The CS is repeatedly presented without the UCS
questions.explanation: Extinction weakens the conditioned response when the CS is presented repeatedly without the UCS.

questions.question: Elaborative rehearsal improves long-term memory retention compared to maintenance rehearsal because it:
questions.explanation: Elaborative rehearsal connects new information to existing knowledge, creating richer encoding and better retrieval cues.

questions.question: The framing effect demonstrates that decision-making is influenced by:
options: How information is presented rather than its content
questions.answer: How information is presented rather than its content
questions.explanation: Tversky and Kahneman showed that equivalent information framed as gains vs. losses leads to different decisions.

questions.question: Sternberg's triarchic theory divides intelligence into analytical, creative, and:
questions.explanation: Sternberg's three intelligences are analytical (academic), creative (novel problem-solving), and practical (everyday skills).

questions.question: The Schachter-Singer two-factor theory of emotion proposes that emotion = physiological arousal +:
questions.explanation: Two-factor theory holds that we experience emotion when we apply a cognitive label to physiological arousal.

questions.question: In Piaget's preoperational stage, children typically demonstrate:
questions.explanation: Preoperational children (2–7 years) are egocentric — they cannot easily take another person's perspective.

questions.question: Puberty in girls is typically initiated by the release of:
options: Estrogen from the ovaries and adrenal glands
questions.answer: Estrogen from the ovaries and adrenal glands
questions.explanation: Rising estrogen levels trigger the development of secondary sex characteristics and menarche in females.

questions.question: Freud's defense mechanism of repression involves:
options: Attributing one's feelings to another person
Blocking threatening thoughts from conscious awareness
Channeling impulses into socially acceptable behaviors
Reverting to earlier stages of development
questions.answer: Blocking threatening thoughts from conscious awareness
questions.explanation: Repression is the unconscious exclusion of anxiety-provoking thoughts and memories from awareness.

questions.question: Social facilitation refers to the tendency for performance to:
options: Decrease in the presence of others
Improve on well-learned tasks in the presence of others
Improve on novel tasks with an audience
questions.answer: Improve on well-learned tasks in the presence of others
questions.explanation: Zajonc's social facilitation: others' presence improves performance on easy/well-practiced tasks but hurts performance on difficult ones.

questions.question: Which anxiety disorder is characterized by persistent, uncontrollable worry about multiple life domains?
questions.explanation: GAD involves excessive, hard-to-control worry about various areas of life lasting at least six months.

questions.question: The biopsychosocial model of mental health holds that psychological disorders result from:
options: The interaction of biological, psychological, and social factors
questions.answer: The interaction of biological, psychological, and social factors
questions.explanation: The biopsychosocial model integrates genetics, thought patterns, and social environment to explain mental health and illness.

questions.question: Which research method allows researchers to establish cause-and-effect relationships?
questions.explanation: Only experiments, with random assignment and controlled variables, can establish causal relationships.

questions.question: The resting potential of a neuron is approximately:
questions.explanation: At rest, a neuron maintains a membrane potential of approximately -70 millivolts due to ion distribution.

questions.question: Signal detection theory accounts for the role of _______ in perception.
questions.explanation: Signal detection theory shows that perception involves both sensory sensitivity and decision criteria influenced by expectation and motivation.

questions.question: Narcolepsy is characterized by:
options: Sudden onset of REM sleep during waking hours
questions.answer: Sudden onset of REM sleep during waking hours
questions.explanation: Narcolepsy involves sudden, uncontrollable sleep attacks and cataplexy due to dysregulation of REM sleep mechanisms.

questions.question: Spontaneous recovery in conditioning refers to:
options: Acquisition of a new conditioned response
Reappearance of an extinguished response after a rest period
questions.answer: Reappearance of an extinguished response after a rest period
questions.explanation: Spontaneous recovery is the reemergence of an extinguished conditioned response following a period of no exposure.

questions.question: The testing effect refers to the finding that:
options: Tests increase anxiety and reduce learning
Repeatedly testing oneself improves long-term retention
Multiple choice tests are less effective than essays
Studying from notes is superior to flashcards
questions.answer: Repeatedly testing oneself improves long-term retention
questions.explanation: The testing (retrieval practice) effect shows that actively recalling information strengthens memory more than re-reading.

questions.question: The representativeness heuristic leads people to judge probability based on:
options: How closely something matches a typical example
questions.answer: How closely something matches a typical example
questions.explanation: The representativeness heuristic judges probability by similarity to a prototype, often ignoring base rate information.

questions.question: Crystallized intelligence tends to _______ with age, while fluid intelligence tends to _______.
questions.explanation: Crystallized intelligence (accumulated knowledge) grows with age; fluid intelligence (novel problem-solving) peaks in early adulthood and declines.

questions.question: Which theory proposes that people compare themselves to others to evaluate their opinions and abilities?
questions.explanation: Festinger's social comparison theory states that people determine their worth by comparing themselves to similar others.

questions.question: Erikson's stage for young adults centers on the conflict of:
questions.explanation: In young adulthood, Erikson's sixth stage involves the challenge of forming close, committed relationships vs. remaining isolated.

questions.question: Locus of control refers to:
options: The brain region controlling voluntary movement
The belief about whether outcomes are controlled internally or externally
questions.answer: The belief about whether outcomes are controlled internally or externally
questions.explanation: Rotter's locus of control is the degree to which people believe they (internal) vs. external forces control life outcomes.

questions.question: Deindividuation in crowds leads to:
options: Decreased self-awareness and increased impulsive behavior
questions.answer: Decreased self-awareness and increased impulsive behavior
questions.explanation: Deindividuation occurs when anonymity in a crowd reduces self-awareness, lowering restraint and increasing impulsive behavior.

questions.question: Bipolar I disorder is distinguished from bipolar II by the presence of:
questions.explanation: Bipolar I requires at least one full manic episode; Bipolar II involves hypomanic (less severe) episodes only.

questions.question: Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT) was originally developed to treat:
questions.explanation: Marsha Linehan developed DBT specifically for BPD, combining CBT with mindfulness and distress tolerance skills.

questions.question: The diathesis-stress model of psychological disorders proposes that disorders arise from:
options: A predisposition combined with environmental stressors
questions.answer: A predisposition combined with environmental stressors
questions.explanation: The diathesis-stress model holds that a genetic or biological vulnerability (diathesis) interacts with stressors to produce disorder.

questions.question: Which memory system allows us to hold and mentally manipulate information for seconds to minutes?
questions.explanation: Working memory (Baddeley's model) is the active system for temporarily holding and manipulating information.

questions.question: Observational learning requires attention, retention, reproduction, and:
questions.explanation: Bandura's four components of observational learning are attention, retention, reproduction, and motivation (incentive to perform).

questions.question: The superego, in Freudian theory, represents:
options: The internalized moral standards and ideals of society
questions.answer: The internalized moral standards and ideals of society
questions.explanation: The superego is the moralistic component of personality containing the internalized rules and ideals learned from parents and society.

questions.question: Which term describes the tendency to attribute one's successes to internal factors and failures to external factors?
questions.explanation: Self-serving bias protects self-esteem by crediting internal factors for success and blaming external factors for failure.

questions.question: Which therapeutic approach emphasizes unconditional positive regard, empathy, and genuineness?
questions.explanation: Carl Rogers' client-centered (person-centered) therapy uses unconditional positive regard, empathy, and congruence to facilitate growth.
